claims.text: Beginning in 2006, a New York psychiatrist named Dr Brian Stone began collecting cases of patients independently dreaming of the same unfamiliar man — round-faced, prominent unibrow, thin lips — eventually documenting 80 cases in his own practice within a single week.

evidence.quote:
in 2006 jeie a woman living in New
York was at her weekly session with her
psychiatrist Dr Brian Stone

she was visited by a man a man
she didn't know she called him this
man

by the end of the week Dr Stone had
documented 80 cases of people seeing
this man in their dreams

claims.rationale: Per the host's own verdict (see c_0008) Dr Brian Stone, 'Genie,' and the entire 80-cases-in-one-week origin are fabrications by Andrea Natella. No such psychiatrist existed; no patients reported these encounters; the story is the engineered seed of a viral marketing campaign.

claims.text: Reports of dreaming of 'This Man' were said to span the globe — Sao Paulo, Rome, Beijing, Barcelona, Stockholm, Paris, New Delhi, Moscow — accumulating to ~2,000 sightings and eventually ~8,000 total reports via the website thisman.org.

evidence.quote: thisman.org

this man had been seen at least 2,000
times

the number of people reported
seeing this man in their dreams was up
to 8,000

claims.rationale: thisman.org is a real website (still up) and submissions did come in from many countries. However the 'reports' are self-selected anonymous web submissions on a website explicitly designed to elicit them — they are not independent observations and many are likely jokes, copies, or post-exposure pareidolia. Counts in the thousands are plausible as web traffic but worthless as evidence of an actual cross-cultural shared dream entity.

claims.text: Within Project Stargate, the CIA ran a sub-program starting in 1991 to investigate whether researchers could communicate with — and implant images into — sleeping subjects via lucid dreaming, and 'This Man' could be the runaway product of such an experiment.

evidence.quote:
the CIA had a sub project within
Stargate lucid dreaming

claims.rationale: Project Stargate (the SRI / CIA-funded remote-viewing program) was real and ran from 1972 to 1995, but its declassified files (released 2017) document remote viewing of geographic locations, not implantation of dream imagery. There is no published evidence of a CIA shared-dream-implantation sub-project. The host explicitly frames this as a 'darkest theory' — i.e., conspiracy, not established fact.

claims.text: Premonition dreams — Lincoln dreaming of his coffin days before his April 1865 assassination, Mark Twain dreaming of his brother in a metal coffin before his death, 20 Titanic passengers cancelling their tickets after dream warnings — recur often enough across history that they cannot be casually dismissed.

evidence.quote:
20
passengers canceled their tickets for
the Titanic after having dreams about
the ship sinking

claims.rationale: The individual stories have a bedrock of real history mixed with embellishment. Lincoln did reportedly tell Ward Hill Lamon about a coffin dream (Lamon's memoir, decades later — uncorroborated). Twain's brother Henry's death after a riverboat boiler explosion is real, the metal-coffin-dream story is from Twain's autobiography. The 'Titanic dream cancellations' figure is anecdotal and shows up in spiritualist literature but lacks a documented source list. The aggregate pattern is real (people often retroactively connect dreams to disasters — base-rate fallacy) but doesn't establish premonition as a phenomenon.

claims.text: On October 19, 1966 in Aberfan, Wales, ten-year-old Eryl Mai Jones told her mother she dreamt the school had been buried by something black; two days later a colliery spoil tip collapsed in a landslide, killing 116 children and 28 adults including Eryl Mai herself — the case that motivated psychiatrist John Barker to found the British Premonitions Bureau.

evidence.quote:
on October 19th 1966 in Aban
a small mining Village in South Wales
10-year-old arol May Jones

Errol May Jones psychiatrist John Barker
heard about the young girl's prediction

claims.rationale: Largely accurate. The Aberfan disaster (Oct 21, 1966) killed 116 children and 28 adults from collapse of colliery spoil tip 7. Eryl Mai Jones did reportedly tell her mother about a 'black' school dream the night before, per parental testimony given to John Barker in his investigation. Barker did found the British Premonitions Bureau in early 1967 in response. The facts are sound; the interpretation as paranormal precognition is what's disputed (one girl's vague dream the day before, against ~1,500 attendees of a mining-village school under a known unstable spoil tip, is consistent with dream coincidence).

claims.text: Two of John Barker's premonitions-bureau correspondents (Alan Hencher and Kathleen Middleton) independently 'predicted' Barker's own death within a year — and Barker did die at age 42 of a brain hemorrhage, after declining to relay Middleton's warning to Robert Kennedy before RFK's June 1968 assassination.

evidence.quote:
RFK was assassinated by
Siran Sirhan

well John
Barker died within a year brain
hemorrhage he was only 42 years old

claims.rationale: John Barker did die of a cerebral hemorrhage in August 1968, aged 44 (host's '42' is slightly off). Hencher and Middleton are real and were among Barker's most prolific correspondents. Whether they 'predicted' his death depends on standards of evidence — Sam Knight's 2022 book 'The Premonitions Bureau' (which the host's account closely tracks) treats their warnings as suggestive but not conclusive. RFK assassination details are correct.

claims.text: Most dreams across cultures and species are negative survival-rehearsal scenarios (chasing/being chased, falling, teeth falling out, public nakedness, being lost) generated by the amygdala while the prefrontal cortex is suppressed — i.e., dreaming is primarily an evolved threat-simulation mechanism rather than a portal to anywhere supernatural.

evidence.quote:
most common dream humans have like all
mammals is being chased

claims.rationale: This is broadly the threat-simulation theory of dreaming (Antti Revonsuo, 2000) and is the leading evolutionary account of dream content. Universal motifs (chase, falling, lost, exam) are well-documented across cultures (Schredl & Hofmann, 2003). Amygdala activation and prefrontal-cortex hypoactivation during REM are confirmed by fMRI studies. The synthesis is mainstream sleep neuroscience.

claims.text: The This Man phenomenon is a confirmed hoax: Italian advertising executive Andrea Natella admitted to fabricating it as a guerrilla-marketing experiment, drawing the 'This Man' face from a sketch of his own father; downloadable flyers seeded the legend in 35 languages, and Vice published a viral article about him in January 2015 that they had to retract the same day.

evidence.quote:
January 15th 2015 Vice published
an article about this
man

Not only was this man a hoax it was
debunked years earlier

the truth is Andrea Nutella the creator
of the this man myth was an advertising
executive

Flyers turned up all over the world in
35 different languages

Nutella later admitted he made
the whole thing up

the picture
of this man is a sketch based on his
father as a young man

claims.rationale: Documented. Andrea Natella (host occasionally garbles the surname as 'Nutella') is a real Italian artist / guerrilla-marketing operator with the firm Guerriglia Marketing. He admitted in 2010 to inventing This Man, and the Vice retraction sequence (article Jan 15 2015, retraction same day) is on record. The 'father sketch' attribution is from his own statements. The hoax is incontrovertible.

dependencies.rationale: There was no Dr Brian Stone in 2006; the origin story is part of the hoax.

claims.text: Even though This Man was fabricated, thousands of people probably did genuinely have dreams about the face — not because of any paranormal entity, but because suggestion and exposure to a memorable image cause people to incorporate it into their own dreams.

evidence.quote:
8,000 people saying they saw him in
their dreams were they lying well
probably not all of them

claims.rationale: Well-supported by sleep psychology. Day residue (Freud's term) — incorporation of recent waking experience into dreams — is a robust finding (Schredl, 2007). After exposure to a vivid, repeated image (especially via flyers / news coverage), some fraction of viewers will dream about it within weeks. So the 8,000-people figure is consistent with normal day-residue dreaming over a several-year viral cycle, no paranormal explanation needed.

claims.text: Lucid dreaming — being consciously aware that one is dreaming, and steering the dream's content — is a real, learnable skill that switches the prefrontal cortex back on inside REM sleep, and is the most enjoyable form of dreaming available.

evidence.quote:
lucid dreaming is a skill
that can be taught

claims.rationale: Lucid dreaming is well-established in sleep research (Stephen LaBerge, Stanford 1980s; Voss et al. fMRI studies show dorsolateral PFC reactivation during lucid REM). Training methods (MILD, WBTB, reality testing) have measured success rates in published trials. The 'skill that can be taught' framing is supported. The 'most enjoyable' is subjective opinion.

claims.text: Dream interpretation as a serious cultural practice goes back at least to 7th-century-BC Mesopotamia (the Assyrian Dream Book in Ashurbanipal's library) and Ancient Egyptian dream priests, with Freud's 1900 The Interpretation of Dreams as the foundation of modern psychoanalytic theory.

evidence.quote:
the famous Assyrian dream
book in the library of King ashab banal
goes back to the 7th Century BC

Sigman Freud wrote a book
called the interpretation of Dreams

claims.rationale: All historically accurate. The Assyrian Dream Book (Iškar Zaqīqu) is preserved in Ashurbanipal's library at Nineveh, 7th century BC. Egyptian dream books (e.g. the Ramesside Dream Book, Chester Beatty Papyrus III) are real artifacts. Freud's Die Traumdeutung was published 1899/1900 and is the foundation of psychoanalytic dream theory.
